text: Translevator robotic abdominal perineal resection for recurrent rectal cancer after low anterior resection. A six-year-old woman underwent trimodality therapy for locally advanced rectal cancer. She was found to have a low pelvic anastomotic recurrence approximately one year after diagnosis, just after the time of ileostomy reversal. This is the area of the anastomotic recurrence with ulceration. Biopsy revealed moderately invasive adenocarcinoma. Cross-sectional imaging with computed tomography and PET scan was obtained and identified an area of high activity on CT PET scan.

The surgeon mobilizes the proximal neorectum laterally and medially during the translevator robotic abdominal perineal resection, carefully identifying the left and right ureters. Adhesions are dissected from the left uterine vessels, posterior uterus, and vagina using sharp and cautery techniques, with the soft adhesions allowing for minimally invasive dissection.

The surgeon completes anterior dissection and proceeds with proximal mesenteric division to enhance retraction of the neorectum. The left ureter, marked by a temporary ureteral catheter, is palpated and identified. Posterior dissection begins, mobilizing the neorectum or colon from the total mesorectal excision plane, with minimal adhesions allowing for minimally invasive management.

The surgeon initiates translevator dissection on the right posterior lateral portion, focusing on the posterior midline near the distal sacrum and coccyx. The dissection progresses into previously radiated tissue, revealing soft tissue edema and increased difficulty. The translevator dissection continues posteriorly to the left and lateral, aiming for a wide circumferential radial margin.

The surgeon continues the posterior translevator dissection, followed by a bimanual examination to confirm the distal dissection level transrectally and transvaginally. The dissection is completed transperineally, and the specimen is extracted transperineally. The final view shows a wide translevator cylindrical excision from both the abdominal and perineal sides.

The surgeon releases retraction on the uterus and occludes the pelvis. The final pathology specimen is displayed, showing internal structures on the left and posterior dissection on the right. The specimen reveals T2 N0 recurrent rectal cancer with no positive lymph nodes and a 1.4 cm circumferential radial margin.
